data.title: Roche lung cancer therapy beats rivals from Amgen, BMS in late-stage trial
data.description:
Roche (RHHBY) unit Genentech announced on Thursday its KRAS G12C inhibitor divarasib outperformed FDA-approved KRAS G12C inhibitors from Amgen (AMGN) and Bristol Myers Squibb (BMY) in a late-stage trial for patients with non-small cell lung cancer.

The head-to-head trial named Krascendo 1 was designed to evaluate divarasib against first-generation KRAS G12C inhibitors, sotorasib or adagrasib, marketed by Amgen (AMGN) and Bristol Myers (BMY) as Krazati and Lumakras, respectively.

Krascendo 1 reached its primary and a key secondary endpoint as divarasib improved progression-free survival and overall survival with a statistically significant and clinically meaningful impact compared to first-generation KRAS G12C inhibitors, the company said.

The 338-subject trial evaluated divarasib monotherapy versus sotorasib or adagrasib as a late-line option for KRAS G12C-mutant NSCLC.

There were no new safety concerns, and the experimental therapy indicated a tolerability profile consistent with its prior trials, Genentech said, adding that it will present Krascendo 1 data at a future medical meeting and share the findings with global regulators.

The U.S. FDA has already granted Breakthrough Therapy Designation and Orphan Drug Designation to divarasib. The next-gen KRAS G12C inhibitor is currently undergoing late-stage clinical development for NSCLC as a single agent and as part of a chemotherapy-free combination regimen.

data.title: TotalEnergies to sell 85% stake in Malaysian gas asset for $350M
data.description:
TotalEnergies (TTE) said Thursday it agreed to sell its 85% non-operated interest in Block 2E offshore Malaysia for $350M to Japanese energy firm Inpex.

The sale, representing a net interest of 8.5% in the Marjoram gas field currently under development, unlocks the value of its minority interest in a non-operated gas project to focus on its operated portfolio and strategic growth opportunities in Malaysia, the company said.

TotalEnergies (TTE) holds operated and non-operated interests in 17 offshore blocks in Malaysia following the divestment and is the country's third-largest gas producer.

Separately, a group of climate activists filed a lawsuit Wednesday against TotalEnergies (TTE) in a French civil court, seeking to obtain ‌environmental documents related to an onshore Nigerian oil asset the company is trying to sell.

Nigerian regulators have not yet approved the sale announced in January of TotalEnergies' (TTE) 10% stake in the asset to a local company.

data.title: U.S. Physical Therapy buys majority stake in a physical therapy practice
data.description:
• U.S. Physical Therapy (USPH) on Thursday said it acquired a majority stake in a physical therapy practice that owns twelve clinics. 
  • USPH acquired a 67% equity interest, with 33% retained by the current owners.  
  • The practice currently generates about 112,000 annual visits and $12M in annual revenue, as per USPH.

data.title: OpenAI finds way to sharply cut inference costs: report
data.description:
OpenAI (OPENAI) has uncovered a method to sharply cut its computing costs related to inference, The Information reported.

Some engineers at the ChatGPT maker told other colleagues they have figured out a way to cut inference costs in half, finding new ways to optimize, the news outlet added, citing a person familiar with the matter. When the techniques were applied to free or non-paid uses of ChatGPT, the number of Nvidia (NVDA) GPUs needed was cut to a couple hundred, the news outlet added.

It is not yet clear how OpenAI achieved the efficiency gains or what the impact will be, given that OpenAI limits how much usage free or non-paid ChatGPT users get. It also isn't clear how long the efficiency gains will last, as larger models are continuously getting developed and require more cost to run.

OpenAI and Nvidia did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

Earlier this month, OpenAI and Broadcom (AVGO) showed off the first processor from their partnership, codenamed Jalapeño. The new chip was designed specifically to run inference for large language models. It was designed by OpenAI, while partners Broadcom (AVGO) and Celestica (CLS) helped industrialize the platform through chip implementation, board, rack system integration, high-performance networking, and scalable production systems.

OpenAI said its AI models accelerated the development of the processor from initial design to production in only nine months.

data.title: Savaria acquires Italy's elevator maker Vipal
data.description:
• Savaria Corporation (SISXF) on Thursday said it acquired all issued and outstanding shares of Vipal S.p.A., a residential lift and elevator manufacturer based in Ferentillo, Italy.
  • The acquisition provides Savaria with a scalable 64,600 sq. ft. factory and vertically integrated elevator manufacturing capabilities in Europe.
  • Vipal generated sales of around €8.2M or C$13.3M for the trailing twelve months.
  • The financial terms of the transaction were not disclosed.
  • Stock up 4.5% in premarket trading.

data.title: Yorkton Equity acquires related-party property management company
data.description:
• Yorkton Equity Group (YEG:CA)  said Thursday it signed a deal to acquire property manager Yorkton Management from CEO and controlling shareholder Ben Lui and a close family member in a $700K all-share transaction.
  • Yorkton expects to issue 4.67M shares at $0.15 per share to fund the acquisition.
  • Following the deal, Ben Lui is expected to own about 72.4% of the company's outstanding shares.
  • The acquisition brings property management for Yorkton's Edmonton, Alberta, investment properties in-house to improve efficiency and retain related profits.

data.title: Sen. Warren urges Fed's Waller to stop work on reserve bank proposal - report
data.description:
Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D-MA) asked Federal Reserve Governor to cease his project to overhaul operations at the central bank's 12 regional banks, according to a media report on Thursday.

In a letter she sent to Waller on Wednesday, Warren contended that his proposal appears to be inconsistent with federal law and suggested that it could chip away at the central bank's independence, Bloomberg News reported on Thursday. She also called the proposal a "thinly veiled" effort to please President Donald Trump.

"I request that you immediately stop all work on this proposal and provide additional information to Congress," she said.

Waller has some oversight power over the 12 branches as chair of the Fed's reserve bank affairs committee, but the regional banks are set up as largely autonomous bodies with control over their day-to-day operations. 

In two speeches this spring, Waller described a plan to consolidate some back-office operations, such as human resources and IT at the regional banks. He said the regional leaders had agreed to a framework for such changes.

Meanwhile, Fed officials and staffers across the system are concerned that the push to consolidate some operating functions could eventually shift power and decision-making toward Washington, potentially increasing political pressure on the Fed.

Warren also claimed in the letter that Waller has already directed Reserve Bank presidents to implement his proposal. "The ability for the Federal Reserve Board to exercise control over all of these functions, including the administrative infrastructure necessary to hire and fire staff, would represent a significant erosion of the Fed’s decentralized structure," she wrote, according to the Bloomberg article.

Her letter comes against a backdrop of Trump's previous attempts to interfere with the Fed's policymakers. First by a pressure campaign of social media posts berating former Fed Chair Jerome Powell for not cutting interest rates, followed by his attempt to fire Fed Governor Lisa Cook and the Department of Justice's criminal probe into Powell.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: AstraZeneca inks kidney therapy deal worth up to $1.77B with China’s CSPC
data.description:
AstraZeneca (AZN) has reached a partnership worth up to $1.77B with CSPC ‌Pharmaceutical Group (CSPCY) to develop and commercialize treatments for kidney diseases, Reuters reported on Thursday, based on a regulatory filing from the Chinese company.

As part of the deal, the Anglo-Swedish drugmaker will have the option to obtain exclusive global ​rights to develop, manufacture, and commercialize a preclinical small ‌nucleic acid drug candidate. Another option will give the company ex-China rights to a second experimental therapy.

In exchange, CSPC (CHJTF) is entitled to $30M upfront and up to approximately $1.74B in additional payments if it meets certain development and commercial milestones. AstraZeneca (AZN) did not immediately respond to a request seeking additional details on the deal.

Western pharma giants are increasingly turning to China to obtain rights to promising drug candidates, drawing scrutiny from lawmakers on Capitol Hill. Earlier this week, the U.S. House Select Committee ​on China launched a national security investigation into five leading drugmakers over their use of clinical trial sites in China.

data.title: NHTSA closes 2022 Tesla “phantom braking” probe
data.description:
The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration has closed a four-year probe into unexpected deceleration of Tesla’s (TSLA) Model 3 and Model Y vehicles with the model years of 2021 and 2022, having found “no pattern of lane departure or collision risk tied to the events.”

Tesla (TSLA) owners complained of “phantom braking,” in which the vehicles decelerated by 10-20 mph over 1-3 seconds at highway speeds. No collisions tied to the deceleration were identified.

The investigation was triggered by a substantial increase in complaints from 99 at the end of 2021 to 314 by the time NHTSA opened the investigation in February 2022.

The “phantom braking” incidents were separate from an alleged defect in the vehicle’s automatic emergency braking, as the underlying cause for the deceleration was tied to Autopilot, Full Self-Driving, and Traffic-Aware Cruise Control that misread the traffic conditions and initiated an unnecessary stop. 

The agency’s review subsequently determined that Tesla’s complaint and field data aligned with the company’s transition in 2021 from a radar-vision system to vision-only sensing. As a result, phantom braking events declined from 45 in 2024 to just 3 in the first half of 2026.

data.title: Dollar Tree restores share buyback authorization to $2.5B
